title: Design Review: A Student Footbridge Project

turns.speaker: Dr Price
turns.text: Right, let's review your footbridge design. You were asked to bridge a fifteen-metre gap across the campus stream. Talk me through the concept you settled on.

turns.speaker: Yasmin
turns.text: We went for a simple beam bridge in the end. We did consider a suspension design, which looks more impressive, but for a fifteen-metre span it would have been unnecessarily complex and expensive.

turns.speaker: Tom
turns.text: Right — the suspension idea was mostly me being ambitious. Yasmin talked me out of it, and she was correct. For this span, a beam is the sensible choice.

turns.speaker: Dr Price
turns.text: I agree entirely. Engineers should never add complexity for its own sake. Now, the material — you chose weathering steel. What was the reasoning?

turns.speaker: Yasmin
turns.text: Two reasons. First, it's strong enough for the loads we calculated. But mainly, weathering steel forms its own protective rust layer, so it never needs painting. That cuts the maintenance cost right down.

turns.speaker: Dr Price
turns.text: Good — low maintenance is a genuine advantage for a bridge no one will inspect very often. Now, I do have a concern about your calculations. The dead load looked fine, but I wasn't happy with how you handled the live load. Tom?

turns.speaker: Tom
turns.text: Yes, I think I see the problem. We calculated for an average crowd of people, but we didn't account for everyone moving in step — a group walking in rhythm could set up a vibration.

turns.speaker: Dr Price
turns.text: Exactly. Pedestrian bridges can sway alarmingly if the crowd's footsteps synchronise. It's a famous failure mode. You'll need to add a damping calculation.

turns.speaker: Yasmin
turns.text: That makes sense. I'd rather add a damper than make the whole beam heavier, which is what Tom first suggested.

turns.speaker: Tom
turns.text: Fair. A damper is more elegant than just bulking up the steel. I'll concede that one.

turns.speaker: Dr Price
turns.text: Good. Now let's assign the revisions. Someone redoes the live-load maths, someone updates the technical drawings, and I'll review your cost estimate. Preferences?

turns.speaker: Tom
turns.text: I'll redo the live-load calculations with the damping factor — it was my section that had the gap, so I should fix it.

turns.speaker: Yasmin
turns.text: And I'll update the drawings. I did them in the software originally, so it's quickest for me to make the changes.

turns.speaker: Dr Price
turns.text: Perfect. I'll go through your costings and flag anything unrealistic. Submit the revised package by the end of week seven, and make sure the drawings are all to the same scale this time.

turns.speaker: Yasmin
turns.text: Same scale throughout, week seven. Understood. Thanks, Dr Price — the point about synchronised footsteps was one we'd completely missed.

questionGroups.instructions_html: Why did the students reject a suspension design?

options.text: It would look too plain for the setting.
It was too complex and costly for the span.
It could not carry the required load.

Write NO MORE THAN TWO WORDS OR A NUMBER for each answer.

questions.prompt_text: To control the vibration, they will add a ______ rather than more steel.
The footbridge must cross a gap of ______ metres.
The revised package is due by the end of week ______.
